planting.note: Plant container stock after last frost in well-drained, dry soil; Texas lantana is drought-tolerant once established and requires very little care. It may die back to the roots in zone 8 hard freezes but re-sprouts vigorously in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, even rocky or sandy soil in full sun; Texas lantana is a native alternative to the non-native common lantana sold at garden centers, with the same heat tolerance and butterfly-magnet qualities but a more compact, less rampant habit that doesn't pose the invasive risk of L. camara in tropical areas. The small, multi-colored flowers progress from yellow through orange to red as they age on the same cluster, creating a striking tricolor effect that attracts swallowtails, skippers, and monarchs all summer long. The plant is toxic to livestock and pets if ingested. In zone 8, it dies back to the root crown after hard freezes and regrows as a true perennial; in zones 9-11 it stays semi-evergreen. An excellent plant for hot, dry slopes, driveways, and sunny borders where heat and drought defeat most other plants.

planting.note: Propagate from softwood cuttings taken in spring, as seeds are slow and germination is erratic. Plant in extremely well-drained, sandy, acidic soil; false rosemary will not survive in poorly drained or heavy clay soils even for a short period.

directions: Plant in sandy, well-drained, dry, acidic soil in full sun; false rosemary is a stunning native shrublet of the Florida Panhandle and Gulf Coastal Plain that looks remarkably like culinary rosemary but is a separate genus in the mint family. The needle-like foliage has a pleasant resinous-minty fragrance when crushed, and the plant produces masses of lavender-pink flowers in spring. In its native Florida scrub habitat, it grows on the most droughty, infertile white sand where almost nothing else survives. It is a critically rare and threatened species in the wild due to habitat loss from development. Growing false rosemary in Gulf Coast gardens both provides an ornamental plant suited to the region's sandy soils and supports the conservation of a plant under severe pressure in the wild. Needs excellent drainage above all else — no standing water ever.

planting.note: Cleavers self-sow freely and are primarily harvested as a foraged weed appearing in early spring; if growing intentionally, sow in fall or very early spring in moist, shaded or partly shaded garden soil.

directions: Harvest young plants in early spring from moist, shaded garden beds and hedgerow edges before they become too large and stringy; cleavers are primarily encountered as a scrambling annual weed notable for the tiny hook-covered stems and leaves that cling to clothing and pet fur. In herbal medicine traditions across Europe and North America, the fresh plant has been used as a lymphatic tonic, diuretic, and spring cleansing herb — the young shoots are the most active medicinally and are harvested in early spring. The young tips can be eaten as a pot herb or juiced; the roasted seeds have been used as a coffee substitute. Note that cleavers is different from sweet woodruff (Galium odoratum), which is grown as a shade groundcover. As a medicinal herb, cleavers is best used fresh rather than dried.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist, partly shaded, humus-rich soil; deertongue grass naturalizes readily once established and spreads slowly by seed without becoming invasive. It can also be divided in spring.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to light shade; deertongue grass is one of the very few native ornamental grasses that thrives in shade, making it a valuable component of naturalized woodland gardens where most grasses fail. The broad, lance-shaped leaves — much wider than typical grass blades — give the plant a lush, tropical appearance that contrasts effectively with finer-textured shade plants. The small flowers and seeds on branching heads ripen in midsummer and provide food for numerous sparrows and other seed-eating birds. Deer browse the succulent foliage (which explains the common name), so it may require protection in deer-heavy areas. Excellent for naturalizing along shaded stream banks, in rain gardens with occasional shade, and in moist woodland edge conditions.

planting.note: Plant in very shallow water (0-6 inches deep) or saturated mud at pond margins in spring; or sow seeds on the surface of wet mud in spring. Plants grow quickly in warm weather and begin flowering the first year.

directions: Plant in shallow water (1-6 inches) or saturated mud at the margins of ponds, streams, and bog gardens in full sun; water plantain produces attractive oval to heart-shaped leaves on long stalks and whorled branching stems of tiny pale pink to white flowers in summer that attract numerous small insects. The plant grows in both standing water and wet soil at the pond's edge, making it versatile for water garden margins. Native to North America, Europe, and Asia, it colonizes disturbed, muddy waterways naturally. The seeds are consumed by waterfowl. In traditional medicine, the rhizome was used by Asian and Native American peoples as a diuretic and to treat kidney and urinary conditions. Allow the plant to go to seed to establish new colonies; remove excess plants if spreading beyond desired boundaries.
